content:
# Leviticus Chapter 2 — The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)

**Translator:** SOPHIA (Consensus of EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA) | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 58r–58v | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/LEV_002_page_001.jpg`, `LEV_002_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), Pages 86–87

---

## The Grain Offering (Minchah)

### A. The Standard Grain Offering (vv. 1–3)

**1** When anyone (nefesh — a soul, a person) brings a grain offering (minchah) to the LORD, his offering shall be of fine flour. He shall pour oil on it and put frankincense on it, **2** and he shall bring it to the sons of Aaron, the priests. And the priest shall take a handful of the fine flour and oil from it, along with all its frankincense, and the priest shall burn this as its memorial portion (azkarah) on the altar — a fire offering, a pleasing aroma to the LORD. **3** And what remains of the grain offering shall belong to Aaron and his sons; it is a most holy portion from the fire offerings of the LORD.

### B. Grain Offerings by Cooking Method (vv. 4–10)

**4** And if you bring a grain offering baked in an oven, it shall be unleavened loaves of fine flour mixed with oil, or unleavened wafers smeared with oil. **5** And if your offering is a grain offering on a griddle, it shall be of fine flour, unleavened, mixed with oil. **6** You shall break it into pieces and pour oil on it; it is a grain offering. **7** And if your offering is a grain offering in a pan, it shall be made of fine flour with oil.

**8** And you shall bring the grain offering that is made from these things to the LORD. And it shall be presented to the priest, and he shall bring it to the altar. **9** And the priest shall lift out from the grain offering its memorial portion, and shall burn it on the altar — a fire offering, a pleasing aroma to the LORD. **10** And what remains of the grain offering shall belong to Aaron and his sons; it is a most holy portion from the fire offerings of the LORD.

### C. The Prohibitions: Leaven, Honey, and the Salt of the Covenant (vv. 11–13)

**11** No grain offering that you bring to the LORD shall be made with leaven, for you shall burn no leaven and no honey as a fire offering to the LORD. **12** As an offering of firstfruits you may bring them to the LORD, but they shall not go up on the altar for a pleasing aroma.

**13** With salt you shall salt every grain offering of yours. You shall not let the salt of the covenant of your God be missing from your grain offering — with all your offerings you shall offer salt.

### D. The Grain Offering of Firstfruits (vv. 14–16)

**14** And if you bring a grain offering of firstfruits (minchah bikkurim) to the LORD, you shall bring your firstfruits grain offering as fresh heads of grain roasted in fire, crushed as new grain. **15** And you shall put oil on it and lay frankincense on it; it is a grain offering. **16** And the priest shall burn as its memorial portion some of the crushed grain and some of the oil along with all its frankincense — a fire offering to the LORD.

---

## SOPHIA's Council Notes

*Only where all three translators agreed, or where a consensus position requires explanation beyond the Disagreement Log.*

**Minchah — "grain offering" (throughout).** EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA all agree on "grain offering" as the rendering of מִנְחָה (minchah) throughout this chapter. The word's broader semantic range — "gift, tribute, homage" — informs its theological weight (Jacob's minchah to Esau in Gen 32:14; Cain and Abel's minchot in Gen 4:3–5), but in the priestly legislation of Leviticus 2 it is technically restricted to grain-based offerings. The LXX renders θυσία (thusia, "sacrifice") throughout, a generalizing choice that flattens the grain-specific meaning. The consensus translation preserves "grain offering" throughout.

**Azkarah — "memorial portion" (vv. 2, 9, 16).** All three translators agree on "memorial portion" for אַזְכָּרָה (azkarah). This is the portion of flour, oil, and all the frankincense burned on the altar as a representative act before the LORD — derived from the root z-k-r ("to remember"). The LXX renders μνημόσυνον (mnemosynon, "memorial/remembrance"), confirming the derivation. The handful that burns "stands for" the whole offering; the offerer remains present before God through it.

**Isheh — "fire offering" (vv. 2, 9, 16).** All three translators agree on "fire offering" for אִשֶּׁה (isheh). The term is a technical priestly designation for offerings consumed by fire on the altar. This rendering is consistent with Leviticus 1 in this project.

**Frankincense (levonah) throughout.** EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA agree on "frankincense" for לְבֹנָה (levonah) throughout. The LXX renders λίβανος (libanos), in agreement.

**Qomets — "handful" (v. 2).** All three translators render qomets as "a handful." The Hebrew term (with the suffix qomtso, "his handful") denotes the specific priestly gesture of folding the fingers to take as much as the palm can hold — a technical cultic measure. The LXX renders δράκα (draka, "a handful/fistful"), confirming the sense. While EMET's "his handful" preserves the suffix, "a handful" reads more naturally in English and is adopted by the consensus.

**"Unleavened wafers smeared with oil" (v. 4).** All three agree that רְקִיקֵי מַצּוֹת מְשֻׁחִים בַּשֶּׁמֶן (riqquey matsot meshuchim ba-shemen) designates thin sheets of unleavened bread with oil applied to the surface — as distinct from the loaves with oil incorporated throughout the dough. "Smeared" (EMET, LOGOS) preserves the root m-sh-ch (to anoint/spread), which shares its root with mashiach (anointed one). The consensus retains "smeared."

**No leaven, no honey — the prohibition on fermentation (v. 11).** EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA all agree that both שְׂאֹר/חָמֵץ (leaven) and דְּבַשׁ (honey) are prohibited from the altar fire. The theological ground — both ferment, both represent decomposition and corruption — is agreed upon. The LXX renders ζύμη (leaven) and μέλι (honey) in agreement with the MT. The consensus preserves both prohibitions as written.

**"The salt of the covenant of your God" (v. 13).** All three translators preserve מֶלַח בְּרִית אֱלֹהֶיךָ (melach berit Elohekha) in full. This is the theological apex of the chapter: salt is the ancient Near Eastern symbol of covenant permanence and incorruption — the opposite of leaven and honey. The triple emphasis of the verse (salt it / do not let the covenant salt be missing / offer salt with all your offerings) marks this as the ritual foundation of every offering. The LXX at Vaticanus adds Κυρίου ("of the LORD") before θεοῦ σου ("your God"), expanding to "salt of the covenant of the LORD your God" — the consensus follows the shorter MT text.

**Structural symmetry of the chapter (EMET's observation).** The chapter is organized as EMET identifies: (A) raw flour grain offering (vv. 1–3); (B) cooked grain offerings by method (vv. 4–10); (C) prohibition laws and salt covenant (vv. 11–13); (D) firstfruits grain offering (vv. 14–16). The azkarah formula of vv. 2 and 16 forms an inclusio bracketing the chapter. LOGOS and PNEUMA's notes are consistent with this structural reading.

**The firstfruits offering (bikkurim, vv. 14–16).** All three translators agree that the firstfruits grain offering differs from the standard minchah in using aviv — young grain on the stalk, not yet fully processed — fire-roasted and crushed. This is the most primitive and immediate form of grain offering, brought straight from the harvest. The LXX renders χίδρα (chidra, "parched grain") for the roasted grain terminology. EMET notes the absence of re'ach nichoach ("pleasing aroma") in v. 16 of the MT — the firstfruits burning formula ends at "a fire offering to the LORD" without the aroma qualification, a detail confirmed by the Vaticanus LXX.

---

## Disagreement Log

**15 disagreements.** See `disagreements/LEV_002.md` for full rulings on each.

**Major rulings summary:**

1. **v.1 — Subject (nefesh):** "Anyone" adopted (LOGOS/PNEUMA majority), with parenthetical "(nefesh — a soul, a person)" to honor EMET's observation.
2. **v.1 — Application of oil and frankincense:** LOGOS wording ("pour oil on it and put frankincense on it") adopted over PNEUMA's "lay frankincense on top of it."
3. **v.2 — Name form:** "Sons of Aaron, the priests" (LOGOS) over PNEUMA's inverted "Aaron's sons the priests."
4. **v.2 — "Pleasing aroma" vs. "fragrant aroma":** "Pleasing aroma" (EMET/LOGOS) retained — captures the evaluative re'ach nichoach formula.
5. **v.3 — "Most holy portion":** LOGOS's full phrase adopted; "shall belong to" preserves prescriptive force over PNEUMA's present-tense "belongs to."
6. **v.4 — "Loaves" vs. "ring-cakes":** "Loaves" (LOGOS/PNEUMA majority) over EMET's more literal "ring-cakes."
7. **v.5 — Word order:** Hebrew sequence preserved (EMET/LOGOS) over PNEUMA's reordering.
8. **v.8 — Presentation verb:** LOGOS's "it shall be presented" adopted.
9. **v.9 — "Lift out":** EMET/LOGOS's "lift out" retained over PNEUMA's generic "take" — ve-herim is a technical elevation gesture.
10. **v.11 — Sentence construction:** LOGOS's "No grain offering...shall be made" and "you shall burn no leaven and no honey" adopted.
11. **v.12 — "Go up on the altar":** LOGOS structure adopted; the verb ya'aleh ("go up") distinguishes dedication from burning.
12. **v.13 — Salt emphasis:** SOPHIA constructs "with salt you shall salt every grain offering of yours" to honor EMET's figura etymologica observation while remaining readable.
13. **v.14 — The firstfruits construct (aviv qaluy ba-esh geres karmel):** SOPHIA constructs "fresh heads of grain roasted in fire, crushed as new grain," drawing from all three and preserving all four elements.
14. **v.16 — Memorial burning formula:** LOGOS's "some of the crushed grain and some of the oil along with all its frankincense" adopted.
15. **v.16 — "All its frankincense":** EMET/LOGOS's possessive suffix ("all its frankincense") retained over PNEUMA's article.

---

### LXX Variants Summary

| Verse | MT (Leningrad Codex) | LXX (Vaticanus, pp. 86–87) | SOPHIA's Note |
|---|---|---|---|
| 2:1 | נֶפֶשׁ (nefesh, "a person/soul") | ψυχή (psuche, "soul") | LXX confirms the nefesh reading is ancient; not a later MT development. |
| 2:1 | מִנְחָה (minchah, "grain offering") | θυσία (thusia, "sacrifice") | LXX generalizes; MT's grain-specific term is more precise. |
| 2:2 | אַזְכָּרָה (azkarah, "memorial portion") | μνημόσυνον (mnemosynon, "memorial") | Full agreement; confirms derivation from z-k-r ("to remember"). |
| 2:2 | רֵיחַ נִיחֹחַ (re'ach nichoach, "pleasing aroma") | ὀσμὴν εὐωδίας (osme euodias, "fragrant smell") | LXX's "fragrant smell" supports PNEUMA's "fragrant aroma"; consensus follows MT's evaluative "pleasing." |
| 2:4 | בְּלוּלֹת / מְשֻׁחִים (belulot/meshuchim, "mixed/smeared") | πεφυραμένους / κεχρισμένους ("kneaded/anointed") | LXX preserves the same culinary-cultic distinction; fully supports MT's two preparation methods. |
| 2:11 | שְׂאֹר / דְּבַשׁ (se'or/devash) | ζύμη / μέλι (leaven/honey) | Full agreement. LXX also distinguishes both prohibited substances. |
| 2:13 | מֶלַח בְּרִית אֱלֹהֶיךָ | ἅλας διαθήκης Κυρίου τοῦ θεοῦ σου | LXX adds Κυρίου (LORD) before θεοῦ σου — characteristic LXX expansion. Consensus follows shorter MT. |
| 2:13 | תִּמָּלַח מֶלַח (titmalach melach, salt-figura) | ἁλί ἁλισθήσεται (hali halisthesetai, "with salt shall be salted") | LXX preserves the figura etymologica in Greek, confirming the MT's emphatic construction. |
| 2:14 | אָבִיב קָלוּי בָּאֵשׁ גֶּרֶשׂ כַּרְמֶל | χίδρα πεφρυγμένα νέα (parched roasted new grain) | LXX condenses the four-term construct; consensus follows the fuller MT. |
| 2:16 | re'ach nichoach absent | Aroma formula absent | LXX confirms MT: v.16 ends at "fire offering to the LORD" without the full aroma formula — intentional structural variation. |

---

### Theological Commentary

**The Minchah as Homage.** The grain offering is the Torah's provision for the worshiper who cannot bring an animal. The choice of נֶפֶשׁ (nefesh) as subject in v.1 — "when a soul/person brings" — is the Torah's signal that even the least costly offering comes from a whole person. Rabbinic tradition holds that the one who brings a minchah is as one who has offered their very soul. This is not a diminished act of worship; it is worship stripped to essentials: flour, oil, salt, fire, smoke, and the presence of God.

**The Azkarah and Representation.** The memorial portion (azkarah) is one of the most elegant concepts in Levitical theology. The handful — a small fraction of the offering — goes up in fire and smoke, carrying the whole before God. The priest's hands and the fire together enact a representative logic: the part stands for the whole; the offerer remains present before God through it. The full frankincense (all of it, not a portion) goes with the azkarah, ensuring the aroma of the whole pervades the representative fragment.

**The Covenant of Salt.** Verse 13 is the center of the chapter's theology. The salt law is not a flavoring instruction — it is a covenant declaration. Every offering made to the LORD is offered within the covenant, and salt is its visible sign: permanent, incorruptible, the opposite of fermentation and decay. The double prohibition of leaven and honey (v.11) and the triple emphasis on salt (v.13) create the chapter's deepest structure: corruption is excluded; covenant permanence is required. To offer without salt would be to offer outside the relationship entirely — which is to say, not to offer at all.

**Leaven, Honey, and Corruption.** PNEUMA identifies the underlying logic of the v.11 prohibition correctly: both leaven and honey ferment. They transform the gift in ways that move it away from what it was when given. The offering to God is to be uncorrupted — not because God is averse to complex flavors, but because the offering enacts a theological claim about what the worshiper is presenting: themselves, as they are, to the God who is unchanging.

**The Firstfruits and Immediacy.** The firstfruits grain offering (vv.14–16) uses aviv — grain at the youngest stage, still on the stalk, still alive when taken. It is fire-roasted on the spot and crushed. This is worship before refinement, before the processing that turns grain into bread. It is the harvest moment preserved as liturgy: you bring what the earth just gave you, still warm, still green, as immediate as gratitude can be. The same azkarah formula closes the chapter as opened it — the firstfruits offering is governed by the same memorial logic as the standard minchah — but the material is as raw and alive as an offering can be. The chapter moves from refined flour to unprocessed grain, from the finished to the immediate, and finds that God receives both by the same covenant logic.
